# Improving sampling: fixing an over-specified model

Sometimes the chains misbehave — sky-high R-hat, tiny ESS, trace plots that
wander across enormous ranges or drift apart and never settle. The first
instinct is "the sampler is broken", but far more often the model is asking the
data to estimate something it **cannot**. This is an *over-specified* model, and
the cure is to respecify it, not to sample harder.

## The data

We use `random-effects.csv` — the benthic-cover survey from the same Kia
Province reefs you met with the fish counts. Each row is one transect, and
`cover` is the per-cent cover of branching *Acropora* coral. There are several
transects per site, so `site` is a natural grouping variable for random effects.

## A broken model

Here is a model that tries to give every site its own free mean, on top of a
global intercept, with very diffuse priors:

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    cover[i] ~ dpois(lambda[i])
    log(lambda[i]) <- alpha + beta[site[i]]
  }
  alpha ~ dnorm(0, 1000)
  for (j in 1:J) {
    beta[j] ~ dnorm(0, 1000)
  }
}
```

Set `J` to the number of sites (49) alongside `N` in the constants, then fit it.
The diagnostics will be dreadful: R-hat for `alpha` and the `beta[j]` will be
huge, and ESS will collapse to single digits.

## Why it fails: non-identifiability

Look at the linear predictor: $\log(\lambda_i) = \alpha + \beta_{\text{site}[i]}$.
You can add any constant $c$ to $\alpha$ and subtract the same $c$ from *every*
$\beta_j$, and the predictions are completely unchanged:

$$
(\alpha + c) + (\beta_j - c) = \alpha + \beta_j
$$

So the data can pin down each sum $\alpha + \beta_j$, but never $\alpha$ and the
$\beta_j$ separately — there are infinitely many equally good answers along a
ridge. The two diffuse `dnorm(0, 1000)` priors do nothing to break the tie, so
the sampler slides up and down that ridge forever. That is exactly what high
R-hat and tiny ESS are telling you.

## The fix: partial pooling and sensible priors

Make the site effects deviations around the global mean, drawn from a shared
distribution whose width $\sigma_b$ the model estimates. Anchoring them at zero
identifies $\alpha$ as the grand mean, and the shared prior pools information
across sites — exactly the random-effects idea from Modules 18–19:

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    cover[i] ~ dpois(lambda[i])
    log(lambda[i]) <- alpha + b[site[i]]
  }
  for (j in 1:J) {
    b[j] ~ dnorm(0, sigma.b)
  }
  alpha   ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  sigma.b ~ dunif(0, 10)
}
```

Two changes did the work:

1. **Identifiability** — the `b[j]` are centred on 0 with a shared, estimated SD, so they can no longer trade off freely against `alpha`.
2. **Sensible priors** — `dnorm(0, 10)` on the log-scale intercept and a bounded `dunif(0, 10)` on $\sigma_b$ are weakly informative rather than absurdly vague.

Refit and watch R-hat fall toward 1.0 and ESS climb into the hundreds.

## Your task

1. Fit the **broken** model and read the worst R-hat and minimum ESS from the **Summary** tab.
2. Fix it with the partial-pooling version above (adjust priors if you like).
3. Refit and record the **before/after** worst R-hat and minimum ESS in the table below.
